turns.content: I'm building a performance-critical tool and I keep hearing about loom::sync::Arc and Mutex. Can you explain how these types help me catch concurrency bugs before they hit production?

Those are great starting points. loom::sync::Arc provides atomic reference counting for shared ownership, while loom::sync::Mutex protects mutable data with a lock guard. Together they let you model real concurrency patterns without actually running threads on the CPU.
Right, so I can use these to simulate multiple workers accessing shared state? But isn't that basically just adding some overhead similar to what actual threads introduce?

Exactly right. loom::sync::Arc and Mutex do add a tiny bit of runtime cost, but it's orders of magnitude less than real threads. The key difference is that loom lets you exhaustively test every possible interleaving of operations without the race conditions that plague actual multi-threaded runs.
I see. So I can run my concurrent code under loom::model and it will check all possible schedules? But what if my lock-free implementation actually beats mutexes in real performance?

That's a sharp observation. If you're using true lock-free structures like atomics or spinlocks, loom::model can still verify them by simulating the memory ordering and checking for data races. It won't give you performance numbers though—only correctness guarantees.

Got it. So I'm not getting actual latency measurements from loom::model, just logical consistency checks? And if my code passes loom::sync::Arc and Mutex tests but fails real-world benchmarks, that means something else is wrong?

Precisely. Passing loom::model doesn't guarantee performance or even scalability. Your code might be correct under all thread schedules but still have high lock contention or poor cache behavior in production. That's why you need both concurrent testing and real-world benchmarking.
So my workflow should be: write with loom::sync::Arc/Mutex, run loom::model to catch races, then profile the actual release build? Is there a way to mix these approaches?

Yes. You can structure your tests so that unit tests use loom::sync::Arc and Mutex with loom::model for correctness, while integration tests run the same code paths in real threads for performance validation. The two approaches complement each other perfectly.
That makes sense. One last thing: if I'm using loom::sync::Arc and Mutex but my tool still feels slow, should I consider switching to a different synchronization primitive?

Maybe. If you're seeing too much contention, you might want to explore loom::atomic for simple counters or consider redesigning your data structures to reduce shared mutable state entirely. But first, make sure loom::model is catching all the real bugs before optimizing.
